content: If $f: X \rightarrow Y$ be a function defined by $f(x)=a \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)+b \cos x+c$ and $f$ is bijective, then the set $X$ with $\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{a+\sqrt{2} b}{a}\right)$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { We have, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & f(x)=a \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)+b \cos x+c \\ & f(x)=\frac{a \sin x}{\sqrt{2}}+\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}+b\right) \cos x+c=r \sin (x+\theta)+c \\ & \text { where } r=\sqrt{\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2+\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}+b\right)^2} \text { and } \\ & \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{a+\sqrt{2} b}{a}\right) \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

For $f$ to be one-one, we must have

$$ \begin{aligned} & -\frac{\pi}{2} \leq x+\theta \leq \frac{\pi}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow-\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{2}-\theta \Rightarrow x \in\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta, \frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right] \end{aligned} $$

Hence, $X=\left[\frac{-\pi}{2}-\theta, \frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right]$, where

$$ \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{a+\sqrt{2} b}{a}\right) $$

content: If the function $f: R \rightarrow R$ is defined by $f(x)=x^2+5 x+9$, then $f^{-1}(9)$ is equal to
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\\ &\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } f^{-1}(9)=x \\ & \Rightarrow \quad f(x)=9 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x^2+5 x+9=9 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x^2+5 x=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x(x+5)=0 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & & x & =0,-5 \text { which are in } R . \\ \therefore & & f^{-1}(9) & =\{0,-5\} \end{aligned} $$

content: Let $ [x] $ denote the greatest integer $ \leq x $. If $ f(x)=[x] $ and $ g(x)=|x| $, then the value of $ f\left(g\left(\frac{8}{5}\right)\right)-g\left(f\left(-\frac{8}{5}\right)\right) $ is
explanation:
Given that $ f(x) = [x] $ represents the greatest integer less than or equal to $ x $, and $ g(x) = |x| $, let's solve the expression $ f\left(g\left(\frac{8}{5}\right)\right) - g\left(f\left(-\frac{8}{5}\right)\right) $.

First, calculate the individual components:

Calculate $ f\left(-\frac{8}{5}\right) $:

$ f\left(-\frac{8}{5}\right) = \left[-\frac{8}{5}\right] = -2 $

Calculate $ g\left(\frac{8}{5}\right) $:

$ g\left(\frac{8}{5}\right) = \left|\frac{8}{5}\right| = \frac{8}{5} $

Now, substitute these values into the expression:

Evaluate $ f\left(g\left(\frac{8}{5}\right)\right) $:

$ f\left(g\left(\frac{8}{5}\right)\right) = f\left(\frac{8}{5}\right) = \left[\frac{8}{5}\right] = 1 $

Evaluate $ g\left(f\left(-\frac{8}{5}\right)\right) $:

$ g\left(f\left(-\frac{8}{5}\right)\right) = g(-2) = |-2| = 2 $

Finally, calculate the expression:

$ f\left(g\left(\frac{8}{5}\right)\right) - g\left(f\left(-\frac{8}{5}\right)\right) = 1 - 2 = -1 $

Hence, the value of the expression is $-1$.

content: If $$f(x)=x^2-2 x+1$$ and $$f \circ g(x)=x^2+2 x+1$$, then $$g(x)$$ is equal to
explanation:
We have, $$f(x)=x^2-2 x+1$$ and

$$\begin{array}{ll} & f \circ g(x)=x^2+2 x+1 \\ \therefore & f(g(x))=x^2+2 x+1 \\ \Rightarrow & (g(x))^2-2\left(g(x)+1=x^2+2 x+1\right. \\ \Rightarrow & (g(x))^2-2\left(g(x)-x^2-2 x=0\right. \\ \Rightarrow & g(x)=\frac{-(-2) \pm \sqrt{(-2)^2-4\left(-x^2-2 x\right)(1)}}{2(1)} \\ \Rightarrow & g(x)=\frac{2 \pm \sqrt{4+4 x^2+8 x}}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & g(x)=\frac{2 \pm 2 \sqrt{x^2+2 x+1}}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & g(x)=1 \pm(x+1) \\ \Rightarrow & g(x)=x+2-x \end{array}$$

content: If g(x) = x2 + x $$-$$ 2 and $$\frac{1}{2}gof(x)=2x^2-5x+2$$, then f(x) is equal to
If f(x) = 4x $$-$$ x2, x$$\in$$R, and f(a + 1) $$-$$ f(a $$-$$ 1) = 0, then a is equal to
The maximum value of the function y = x(x $$-$$ 1)2, is
explanation:
We have, $$y = x{(x - 1)^2}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${{dy} \over {dx}} = {(x - 1)^2}\,.\,1 + 2x(x - 1) = 3{x^2} - 4x + 1 = (x - 1)(3x - 1)$$

For maximum or minimum, $${{dy} \over {dx}} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (x - 1)(3x - 1) = 0 \Rightarrow x = {1 \over 3},1$$

Now, $${{{d^2}y} \over {d{x^2}}} = 6x - 4 \Rightarrow {\left[ {{{{d^2}y} \over {d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = {1 \over 3}}} = - 2 < 0$$

$$\therefore$$ y is maximum when $$x = {1 \over 3}$$ and maximum value is

$${[y]_{x = {1 \over 3}}} = {4 \over {27}}$$

Also, $${\left[ {{{{d^2}y} \over {d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = 1}} = 2 > 0$$

$$\therefore$$ y is minimum when x = 1.

content: Find the area enclosed by the loop in the curve 4y2 = 4x2 $$-$$ x3.
explanation:
Substitute 0 for y in the equation 4y2 = 4x2 $$-$$ x3,

0 = x2(4 $$-$$ x) $$\Rightarrow$$ x = 0, x = 4

It means curve makes the loop symmetric about X-axis between 0 and 4.

Area $$ = 2\int_0^4 {ydx = {2 \over 2}\int_0^4 {\sqrt {4{x^2} - {x^3}} dx = \int_0^4 {x\sqrt {4 - x} \,dx} } } $$

Let $$4 - x = t \Rightarrow - dx = dt$$

Area $$ = - \int_4^0 {(4 - t)\sqrt t \,dt} $$

$$ = \int_0^4 {(4\sqrt t - t\sqrt t )dt = \left[ {4 \times {2 \over 3}{t^{3/2}} - {2 \over 5}{t^{5/2}}} \right]_0^4} $$

$$ = {8 \over 3} \times 8 - {2 \over 5} \times 32 = {{64} \over 3} - {{64} \over 5} = 64 \times {2 \over {15}} = {{128} \over {15}}$$

content: If $$2f(xy) = {(f(x))^x} + {(f(y))^x}$$ for all $$x,y \in R$$ and $$f(1) = a( \ne 1)$$. Then $$\sum\limits_{k = 1}^n {f(k) = } $$
explanation:
We have, $$2f(xy) = {(f(x))^x} + {(f(y))^x}$$, $$x,y \in R$$ and $$f(1) = a$$.

Putting x = 1, y = 2, we get

$$2f(2) = {(f(1))^2} + f(2) \Rightarrow f(2) = {a^2}$$

Putting x = 1, y = 3, we get

$$2f(3) = {(f(1))^3} + f(3) \Rightarrow f(3) = {a^3}$$, and so on.

$$\therefore$$ $$f(n) = {a^n}$$

$$\sum\limits_{k = 1}^n {f(i) = a + {a^2} + .. + {a^n} = {{a({a^n} - 1)} \over {a - 1}}} $$.

content: Let f(x) = x $$-$$ 3, g(x) = 4 $$-$$ x. Then the set of values of x for which $$|f(x) + g(x)|\, < \,|f(x)| + |g(x)|$$ is true, is given by :
explanation:
The given inequality is equivalent to

$$1 < |x - 3| + |4 - x|$$

i.e., $$1 < |x - 3| + |x - 4|$$ ...... (i)

For x < 3, we have

$$3 - x + 4 - x = 7 - 2x > 1$$

i.e., x < 3, which is true in the domain.

For $$3 \le x < 4$$, Eq. (i) gives

$$x - 3 + 4 - x = 1 < 1$$ ..... (ii)

For 4 $$\le$$ x, we have

$$2x - 7 > 1 \Rightarrow x > 4$$ ...... (iii)

From Eqs. (i) and (iii), we have the solution of the inequality as x < 3 or x > 4.

i.e., $$x \in ] - \infty ,3) \cup (4,\infty $$ [which is equal to R $$-$$ [3, 4].

Given, $${{|x - 2|\, - 1} \over {|x - 2|\, - 2}} \le 0$$

Let $$|x - 2|\, = k$$

Then, given equation,

$${{k - 1} \over {k - 2}} \le 0 \Rightarrow {{(k - 1)(k - 2)} \over {{{(k - 2)}^2}}} \le 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (k - 1)(k - 2) \le 0 \Rightarrow 1 \le k \le 2$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 \le |x - 2|\, \le 2$$

Case I When $$1 \le |x - 2|$$

$$ \Rightarrow |x - 2| \ge 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow x - 2 \ge 1$$ or $$x - 2 \le - 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow x \ge 3$$ and $$x \le 1$$ ..... (i)

Case II When $$|x - 2| \le 2$$

$$ \Rightarrow - 2 \le x - 2 \le 2$$

$$ \Rightarrow - 2 + 2 \le x \le 2 + 2$$

$$ \Rightarrow 0 \le x \le 4$$ ..... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), $$x \in [0,1] \cup [3,4]$$.
